chunk_summary: Guidelines for macronutrient decisions in toddlers (1–3 years) emphasize energy-dense meals, adequate fat, high-quality protein, and avoiding excessive milk or snacks. Practical strategies for poor appetite and escalation criteria are included.
learning_objectives: Understand the macronutrient needs and energy requirements of toddlers aged 1–3 years.
Identify practical strategies to manage poor appetite and promote balanced nutrition in toddlers.
Recognize pitfalls such as excessive milk consumption or grazing on low-nutrient snacks.
Learn escalation criteria for toddlers with growth or feeding concerns.
key_facts: Toddlers require 3 meals and 2 planned snacks daily, with water offered between meals.
Avoid low-fat diets in 1–3-year-olds unless medically indicated; add energy with oil, ghee, or peanut paste.
Include at least one high-quality protein source daily, such as milk, egg, fish, or cereal–pulse combinations.
Excessive milk intake can displace solids; a practical ceiling is to avoid making milk the main calorie source.
Escalate care if weight-for-height is < -2 SD or > +2 SD, or if there are recurrent wheeze, diarrhea, or feeding disorders.
reference_quote: Toddlers have high energy needs relative to stomach size and commonly oscillate between 'small intake' days and normal days. Clinical decisions should prioritize meal structure and energy density rather than force-feeding.
document_key_topics: Macronutrient needs in toddlers aged 1–3 years
Strategies for managing poor appetite in toddlers
Escalation criteria for growth and feeding concerns
